frameworks.name: Expectation-Experience Alignment
frameworks.definition: The quality of an experience is determined not by objective properties but by the congruence between advance expectations and actual delivery.

claims.text: Whether a product or service is objectively 'good' or 'bad' depends less on its inherent properties and more on what the customer expects it to be like before arriving. The East Berlin hotel was experienced as one of the five best hotels because guests arrived expecting an unconventional experience; had they arrived expecting a Marriott-standard property, the same hotel would have felt like the worst experience of their life.
claims.speaker: Unknown speaker
conditions: Applies to hospitality and service experiences
Applies when customer expectations are set before arrival
When expectations match delivery, satisfaction is high
claims.mechanism: Advance expectation setting anchors the customer's evaluation framework, causing the same objective features to be interpreted as either luxurious or disappointing depending on the comparison set.
bounded_by: May not apply to safety-critical or health-dependent services where objective standards override perception
Does not apply when there is a large gap between minimum acceptable standards and actual delivery

claims.text: Nature of attention shapes nature of experience: the same environment produces radically different subjective outcomes depending on what you are attuned to notice.
claims.speaker: Unknown speaker
conditions: When customers have discretionary choice in how to frame the experience
claims.mechanism: Attention acts as a filter; people selectively encode environmental details that match their advance framework and dismiss those that don't, making the same objective hotel feel luxurious or miserable.
bounded_by: Does not override safety or functional failures (e.g., no running water)
Attention framing works best when underlying conditions meet a minimum threshold

examples.text: A former East Berlin police station or prison, converted into a hotel, featured extremely spartan rooms with a platform bed suspended above a shower, a single black-and-white television tuned to one channel showing The Big Lebowski on continuous loop. The same property was rated as one of the five best hotel experiences when guests arrived expecting an unconventional East Berlin experience, but would have been rated as the worst hotel ever if guests had arrived expecting a standard Marriott property with typical amenities.

practices.text: Set explicit expectations before a customer arrives for a service or experiential product; frame the offering in a way that anchors what the customer should expect to experience, preventing the mismatch that causes dissatisfaction.

Design product positioning and early messaging to establish the evaluation framework customers will use when they arrive; make the frame unusual or distinctive rather than competing on objective features alone.
